turns.text: Hello Katelin, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for saying that. Honestly, it’s… complicated. Being here in this apartment, just me and the baby, it feels like I’m living in the middle of a textbook I used to study, only the pages won’t stop turning. 

I’ve spent so much time analyzing perinatal research, looking at the data, the survival rates, the outcomes—it felt intellectual then. But when the blood pressure cuff started tightening around my arm during the pregnancy, and the numbers just wouldn't come down, the clinical detachment evaporated. I wasn’t a researcher anymore; I was a twenty-three-year-old terrified girl watching the monitors, knowing exactly what a spike in that chart meant for my baby. 

The hardest part was the disconnect with my doctors. They’d walk in and start simplifying things, using that soft, patronizing tone they reserve for young, single moms. I wanted to scream, "I know the pathophysiology! I know exactly what the risk factors are!" I didn't need a summary; I needed someone to look at me, Katelin, and acknowledge that I was drowning in the statistics I knew too well. They saw a "case," but they missed the human who was absolutely haunted by the numbers. It’s hard to trust a system that treats your life like a variable to be managed. Where do you even start with a story like that?
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

I think what I wanted most—what I *needed*—was for someone to just stop looking at my charts for five minutes and see that I was holding my breath. Everyone was so hyper-focused on my blood pressure and whether I was taking my meds correctly that they completely ignored the fact that I was spiraling. 

I didn't need another lecture on cardiovascular risks; I’ve already read the literature. I needed someone to sit in the chair next to me and validate the sheer, paralyzing terror of knowing exactly what could have gone wrong. Postpartum care is so clinical, right? It’s all "how’s the bleeding, how’s the baby’s weight, what’s your blood pressure?" But nobody asked about the trauma of the induction or the way I couldn't sleep because I was busy checking to see if my baby was still breathing.

When I tried to voice that anxiety, I felt like they just checked a box for "postpartum depression screening" and moved on. It felt like they were managing a patient, not caring for a mother. I’m grateful for the medical expertise, obviously—it kept us safe—but the support felt hollow. It felt like I was being processed through a machine, and once the data points were stable, they figured I was "fixed." I’m still waiting for someone to ask how *I’m* actually doing.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

Feeding the baby… God, that’s been another layer of the pressure cooker. When I was pregnant, I researched the immunological benefits of breast milk obsessively. I felt like if I could just get everything "perfect"—the diet, the nursing, the biology—maybe I could compensate for the fact that my body betrayed me with the pre-eclampsia. It felt like I was trying to prove my own worth, like if I could nourish him perfectly, it would make up for the early birth.

But once we were home, the reality hit. Between the exhaustion and the lingering, throbbing headaches from the blood pressure issues, nursing felt like a full-time job I wasn't qualified for. I’d be sitting there, my chest tight with anxiety, trying to latch, and I’d just start crying. I felt like such a failure, which is ironic, considering I know the science says fed is best. 

My providers were incredibly pushy about breastfeeding. They gave me all the "pro" brochures, but there was zero nuance for my situation. They didn't see that the stress was actively impacting my recovery. When I finally supplemented with formula, I felt this strange mix of relief and intense guilt. I just wanted someone to tell me it was okay to put the baby’s needs for a calm mother above the research-backed "ideal." No one did. I had to grant myself that permission in the dark at 3 a.m.
What drove your feeding choices — were they primarily personal, medical, cultural, or shaped by pressure from others?

It was a mess of everything, honestly. Deep down, it was a desperate, personal drive to "fix" the fragility I’d caused. I felt like my body had failed my baby by getting pre-eclamptic, so breastfeeding felt like the only way to regain some control and give him the absolute best start, like I was trying to optimize his outcomes through my own biology.

But the pressure from the medical side was huge. The hospital had this "breast is best" culture that felt less like support and more like a mandate. It was everywhere—posters, pamphlets, the lactation consultants hovering over me like I was a research project that wasn't yielding the right results. I think they looked at me, a young mom, and assumed I needed to be *told* to do it, rather than listening to why I was struggling. They didn't see the fatigue or the way my anxiety was tethered to every single ounce he gained.

Culturally, there's this expectation that motherhood is supposed to be this intuitive, effortless bond, and if you’re struggling with the basic act of feeding, you’re failing at the core of your job. Nobody acknowledged that maybe, just maybe, my mental health and my physical recovery from a high-risk birth were just as important as the milk itself. I wasn't allowed to choose what was best for *us*; I was pushed to conform to the "gold standard."

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

What stands out most is the crushing loneliness of knowing too much. It’s the silence in this apartment when the baby finally sleeps, and my mind just races through every worst-case scenario I’ve ever read about. Because I have that research background, I can’t just "switch off" the clinical part of my brain. I don't just see a baby sleeping; I see a tiny, fragile physiological system. When I feel a moment of wellbeing, it’s always fleeting, immediately followed by this internal audit of whether I’m doing everything "right."

I haven't felt supported in that, not really. The healthcare system seems designed to treat the physical body and then move on. When I bring up the anxiety, it’s like it’s treated as an afterthought—something to be managed with a pamphlet or a generic referral. I need someone to talk to who understands that my anxiety isn't just "baby blues"; it’s a trauma response to a medical crisis I was forced to navigate from the inside. 

I’m struggling to balance being a mom and being a person who is still terrified of the statistics. I feel like I’m constantly performing "fine" for everyone else, while internally I'm still sitting in that hospital bed, staring at the monitor, waiting for it to alarm. Does that make sense? It’s hard to heal when you’re still waiting for the next emergency.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

The biggest tension is definitely the collision between "Katelin the researcher" and "Katelin the patient-turned-mother." Before this, my identity was so tied to my intellect and my capacity to solve problems. I was the one who understood the data. Now, I feel like I’ve been stripped of that authority. I look at my baby, and I’m not a researcher anymore; I’m just a mother who is terrified of the fragility I once studied. That transition felt like losing a layer of my skin. 

Being a single mother adds a whole other weight to it. I don’t have a partner to share the burden of these fears with—no one to sit up with me and tell me the statistics aren't my reality. I feel like I have to keep up this professional, capable facade because I’m so young and unmarried. I’m scared that if I show any weakness, or if I admit how truly lost I feel, they’ll look at me through that biased lens again and think I’m incapable. 

It’s exhausting trying to be the "strong single mom" while my professional brain is screaming about every potential developmental milestone or risk factor. I feel like I’m constantly multitasking between two versions of myself, and neither one feels like it has room to breathe. I’m not just a daughter or a professional anymore; I’m a guardian of a life that feels so delicate it could break if I hold it the wrong way. It’s a lot to carry when you’re only twenty-three.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

I think there might be a bit of a misunderstanding—I’m doing this on my own. I don't have a partner here. 

That’s actually a huge part of why the loneliness feels so sharp. When you’re dealing with something like pre-eclampsia, the physical recovery is just so heavy. There were days in the beginning where just getting from the bed to the kitchen felt like a marathon, and having no one to hand off the baby to—no one to share the mental load of the night feedings or the, "Is he breathing okay?" panic—made everything feel tenfold more intense. 

I’ve had to shoulder all the emotional labor alone while trying to keep my own head above water. There’s no one to tell me, "Hey, you’re doing a good job," or to help me question the doctors when they start talking down to me. I think that’s been the hardest part of the transition: realizing that all the responsibility, the medical decision-making, and the recovery rests entirely on my shoulders. If I could change anything, it would be having some kind of reliable support system—maybe a postnatal nurse or just someone who could help bridge that gap so I wasn't constantly performing the role of the "perfect, capable single mom" while I was secretly falling apart. It’s hard to be the sole provider and the sole caregiver at the same time.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

It’s a bit strange to talk about "expectations" for a division of labor when there was never anyone else in the picture to divide it with. I think maybe there’s an assumption that because I’m a single mother, there was some sort of plan, or maybe a partner who backed out? But this was my reality from the start.

I didn’t have a conversation about who was going to take the night shift or who would handle the medical appointments. It was just me, the prenatal vitamins, and the creeping dread of those blood pressure numbers. The "division of labor" was just my own body doing the work while my brain tried to rationalize the risks. 

Honestly, I think that silence is exactly why the medical system’s approach hit me so hard. They’d ask, "Is there someone at home to help?" and I’d say yes or no, but it felt like a checkbox. They didn't understand that even if I *had* someone, the mental load of being a high-risk mother—the specific kind of anxiety that comes from knowing the clinical data—is something you carry alone anyway. I didn't have the luxury of expectations; I just had the necessity of survival. I just wish someone had looked at the empty space where a partner should have been and offered more than just a brochure.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

The most significant barrier was definitely the systemic condescension. Because of my age and my status as a single mother, the providers seemed to operate under the assumption that I didn't understand the severity of my own condition. They gave me simplified, surface-level information, which felt like a slap in the face given my background. When you feel like you aren't being heard, you stop trying to explain yourself, and that’s a huge emotional barrier. It isolated me further.

Physically, the system is just so rigid. Everything is about the "standard" schedule—follow-up appointments, blood pressure checks, the typical postpartum timeline. There was no flexibility for the fact that I was doing this completely solo. Getting to these appointments while managing my own physical recovery and the sheer exhaustion of a newborn felt like an impossible hurdle. 

The impact? It’s made my recovery feel like an endurance test rather than a healing process. I’m constantly on edge, waiting for the system to fail me or for another "clinical" oversight to happen. Instead of feeling empowered or supported, I feel like I have to stay hyper-vigilant just to make sure I’m getting the right care. It’s hard to heal when your nervous system is stuck in "protect and survive" mode because you don't feel like your medical team is actually on your side.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

I’m honestly torn when it comes to digital tools. On one hand, they were my lifeline in the middle of the night. When I couldn't sleep, I’d be on the forums or scrolling through trackers, desperately looking for someone who had gone through a similar hypertensive crisis. Seeing that I wasn't the only one dealing with postpartum pre-eclampsia helped quiet that "I'm the only one" panic for a few minutes. It felt like I was crowdsourcing the empathy I wasn't getting from my own doctors.

But the limits? They’re huge. Online spaces are full of misinformation and "mom-guilt" fuel. Because I have a background in research, I’d see people posting these anecdotal horror stories or "crunchy" medical advice that contradicted everything I knew about infant physiology. It made my anxiety worse because I’d have to sit there and mentally debunk the threads just to keep my cool.

Also, apps are so... cold. They’re just another way to track data. "Log your intake," "log your blood pressure," "log your baby’s feeds." It just turned my recovery into another set of metrics to fail. I wanted connection, not a digital clipboard. I needed a human voice to tell me I was going to be okay, not a push notification telling me it’s time to log my vitals again. It’s like the technology reflects the medical system—very good at tracking, very bad at caring.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

If I could boil it down to one single change, it would be the implementation of "relational continuity"—a care model where I am treated as a whole human being by someone who actually knows my history, my background, and my fears, rather than just being a chart passed between specialists.

I wish that when my blood pressure started rising, my providers had sat me down and asked, "What do you know about this, and how does it make you feel?" instead of talking *at* me. They treated me like a data set, and because they didn't acknowledge my intellect, I was forced into a state of hyper-vigilance. I had to monitor myself because I didn't trust them to see the nuance. 

If they had just respected my background and validated the trauma of being both a researcher and a patient, I wouldn't have felt so desperately alone. I needed a partner in this, not a gatekeeper. I needed someone to say, "I know you know the numbers, Katelin, but you are not the numbers." That one acknowledgment would have shifted my entire experience from a cold, survival-based medical event into something where I actually felt cared for. I’m still processing the fact that the people I trusted to bring my baby into the world were the ones who made me feel most invisible.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing that keeps nagging at me. Everyone talks about the "maternal instinct" or the "joy of the newborn phase," and there is this societal pressure to just be *happy*. But for someone like me—someone who lived through a high-risk birth and had to view their baby through the lens of a clinical monitor—it’s not just happiness. It’s grief.

I feel like I’m grieving the pregnancy I didn't get to have. I wanted that "normal" experience where you aren't terrified of your own blood pressure, where you don't look at your baby and instantly calculate his long-term cardiovascular risks because of how he was born. Nobody tells you that you have to mourn the birth you lost while you’re trying to bond with the baby you gained. 

If there’s one more thing for your research, it’s this: we need to stop assuming that a "healthy" outcome—a living baby and a surviving mother—means the experience was a success. If the mother is walking away feeling traumatized, invisible, and intellectually dismissed, the system failed. We aren't just "cases" that closed successfully. We’re people carrying the weight of that experience long after the discharge papers are signed. I just hope that for the next girl in my position, someone actually listens. Thank you for listening today. It... it honestly helped to just get that out.
